data.title: Is Intel (INTC) Still Attractively Priced After A 139% One Year Share Price Surge
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Intel's current share price still makes sense after a strong run, this article will help you frame what you are really paying for.
  • Intel's stock last closed at US$46.47, with returns of 3.1% over 7 days, 25.9% over 30 days, 18.0% year to date and a very large 1 year return of 139.2%, plus a 3 year return of 58.9%, while the 5 year return stands at a 12.2% decline.
  • Recent coverage around Intel has focused on its role in the semiconductor industry and its efforts to compete across key markets. This has helped keep investor attention firmly on the stock. Alongside that, commentary on the broader chip sector has added context for how investors think about risk and potential reward for Intel shares.
  • On our 6 point valuation check, Intel scores 3 out of 6, which puts it in the middle of the pack and makes it worth comparing different valuation methods. We will also look at an additional way of thinking about value at the end of the article that can tie everything together.

Intel delivered 139.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Intel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars to arrive at an intrinsic value per share. It is essentially asking what Intel’s projected future cash generation is worth to you right now.

For Intel, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about US$11.5b, and the near term projections reflect a mix of negative and positive cash flows before moving into higher positive territory later in the forecast period. By year 10, the model is using an extrapolated free cash flow estimate rather than direct analyst forecasts.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at US$27.53 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$46.47, this implies the stock is 68.8% above the DCF estimate, which points to a rich valuation on this model.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Intel may be overvalued by 68.8%. Discover 868 undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Intel.

Approach 2: Intel Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are less useful because of cyclical swings or recent losses, the P/S ratio can be a practical way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of revenue. It cuts through short term profit volatility and focuses on the top line that ultimately has to support future profits and cash flows.

What counts as a normal P/S depends on how quickly investors expect revenue to grow and how confident they feel about the risks around that growth. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk usually justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty tend to pull the multiple down.

Intel currently trades on a P/S of 4.39x. The broader Semiconductor industry average is 5.63x, while a selected peer group sits higher at 13.46x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio model, which estimates what Intel’s P/S might be given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk, comes out at 7.09x. This tailored Fair Ratio can be more useful than a straight comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for Intel’s specific characteristics rather than applying a one size fits all benchmark. On this view, Intel’s actual P/S is below the Fair Ratio, which suggests that the shares may be undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover 1417 companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

A Narrative is your own clear storyline for the company, where you spell out what you think happens to its revenue, earnings and margins, and link that to a fair value that you are comfortable with.

Compared with the recent share price of US$133.53, this implies the shares trade at a 57.4% discount to this Excess Returns estimate. This indicates that the stock appears significantly undervalued on this basis.

Approach 2: Popular Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like Popular, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings the business is generating today. It helps you see how many dollars of share price the market is attaching to each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what investors expect for future earnings and how risky they think those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk often justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher perceived risk usually line up with a lower P/E.

Popular currently trades on a P/E of 10.55x. That sits below the Banks industry average of 11.84x and below the peer group average of 12.99x. Since Popular’s Fair Ratio of 13.01x is higher than its current P/E of 10.55x, the shares appear undervalued on this measure.

data.title: Minerals Technologies (MTX) Trailing Loss Challenges Bullish Narrative After Q4 Profit
data.description:
Minerals Technologies (MTX) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$519.5 million and EPS of US$1.19, set against a 12‑month backdrop where trailing revenue sat at about US$2.1 billion and EPS was slightly negative at US$0.59. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$518.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$528.9 million in Q2 2025 and US$532.4 million in Q3 2025 before landing at US$519.5 million in Q4 2025. Over the same period, EPS shifted from US$1.69 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$4.51 in Q1 2025 and then back into the US$1.19 to US$1.44 range. This leaves investors focusing on how consistently the business can convert sales into profit. Margins are front and center here, with the latest numbers highlighting how sensitive overall profitability still is.

See our full analysis for Minerals Technologies.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around Minerals Technologies, and where the numbers start to challenge those stories.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

US$18.4 million trailing loss despite positive recent quarters

  • Over the last 12 months, Minerals Technologies reported a net loss of US$18.4 million even though the last three quarters of FY 2025 showed positive quarterly net income between US$37.1 million and US$45.4 million.
  • What stands out for a bearish view is that, despite those profitable quarters, trailing EPS is still a loss of US$0.59 and losses have grown at about 26.8% per year over five years. This sits awkwardly against the recent quarterly EPS range of roughly US$1.19 to US$1.44 and raises questions about how durable that improvement is.
    • Critics highlight that FY 2025 started with a quarterly loss of US$144 million, which is large relative to the later profits and is a big reason trailing earnings remain negative.
    • They also point out that trailing revenue of US$2.1b is only growing at about 4% per year, so weak earnings are not being offset by strong top line expansion.

Big swings between loss and profit often get value investors interested, but the 5 year earnings decline and US$18.4 million trailing loss keep the cautious camp vocal. 🐻 Minerals Technologies Bear Case

Revenue around US$2.1b, but growth running at 4%

  • On a trailing basis, revenue sits at about US$2.1b, with quarterly revenue over FY 2025 ranging between US$491.8 million and US$532.4 million, and the longer term revenue growth rate quoted at roughly 4% per year compared with a 10.6% US market reference.
  • Supporters taking a more optimistic stance argue that this steady US$2.1b revenue base across multiple quarters, even at 4% annual growth, underpins the business. However, the fact that earnings are still negative despite that scale and growth rate pulls against a simple bullish takeaway.
    • On one hand, quarterly revenue has hovered in a relatively tight band around US$520 million, which can appeal to investors who like consistent sales.
    • On the other hand, with that level of revenue, the trailing net loss of US$18.4 million shows that margins have not yet translated that stability into consistent profits.

DCF fair value at US$124.40 vs US$65.76 share price

  • The DCF fair value is given as US$124.40 per share compared with a current share price of US$65.76 and an analyst price target of US$83.00, so the stock is trading well below both those reference points while its P/S of roughly 1x is below the US chemicals industry at 1.2x and a 1.3x peer average.
  • This apparent discount heavily supports a bullish angle. At the same time, the same data set also shows an unprofitable trailing 12 months and multi year earnings declines of 26.8% per year, so investors weighing the DCF fair value and 83.00 price target against the current US$65.76 price have to balance low sales multiples and those valuation markers against the fact that net income is still a US$18.4 million loss.
    • Analysts pointing to potential upside can lean on both the DCF fair value and the price target being above the current share price while P/S sits below industry and peers.
    • More cautious investors may focus on the negative trailing EPS of US$0.59 and the weak earnings trend as a reason the discount could persist until profitability improves.

The gap between DCF fair value, analyst targets and today’s price is large enough that many investors will want to see how future reports address the US$18.4 million trailing loss before leaning too hard on the apparent discount. 📊 Read the full Minerals Technologies Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Comcast (CMCSA) Margin Strength Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite Flat Revenue Trend
data.description:
Comcast (CMCSA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$32.3b and basic EPS of US$0.60, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$123.7b and EPS of US$5.39. Over the last six reported quarters, revenue has ranged from US$29.9b to US$32.3b, while quarterly EPS has moved between US$0.60 and US$2.99. This gives investors a clear view of how earnings per share translate off a large and relatively steady top line base. With a trailing net margin of 16.2% against 13.1% a year earlier and five year earnings growth in the double digits, the latest numbers frame Comcast as a scale player where the quality of margins sits at the center of the earnings story.

See our full analysis for Comcast.

With the results on the table, the next step is to set these figures against the widely held narratives around Comcast to see which storylines the numbers support and which ones they start to challenge.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins and profit growth outpace modest 0.2% revenue trend

  • Over the last five years, earnings have grown 13.7% per year, with a 23.5% gain in the most recent year, while forecast revenue growth in the data sits at 0.2% per year. This suggests profitability is doing more of the heavy lifting than the top line.
  • What is interesting for the bullish view is that this mix of slow revenue growth and stronger profit growth lines up with a company leaning on a recurring, utility like connectivity base,
    • trailing net margin is 16.2% compared with 13.1% a year earlier, which fits the idea that an established broadband and media group can keep squeezing value out of its existing scale,
    • yet the same bullish argument has to square those profit trends with the very low 0.2% revenue growth figure, which suggests most of the recent strength has come from margins rather than expanding sales.

13.2% forecast EPS decline clashes with 23.5% past growth

  • The data shows trailing earnings growing 13.7% per year over five years and 23.5% over the last year, while analysts in the dataset expect earnings to fall by about 13.2% per year over the next three years.
  • Bears argue that the projected 13.2% annual EPS decline is a key risk,
    • and that view is backed by the explicit forecast for multi year earnings contraction, which sits in sharp contrast to the recent 23.5% one year earnings rise,
    • so anyone leaning bearish can point to that swing from strong historical growth to expected declines as a sign that the last few years of improvement may not repeat in the near term.
Stay alert to how quickly the story could shift if those forecast EPS declines materialize against a backdrop of strong trailing growth and rising margins. 🐻 Comcast Bear Case

P/E of 5.4x and US$77.93 DCF fair value versus US$29.75 price

  • Supporters of a bullish angle point to this combination of low multiple and high DCF fair value,
    • since the P/E discount to both peers and the wider telecom group lines up with the idea that the market is not fully crediting the 16.2% net margin and 13.7% five year annual earnings growth,
